stimulus: A typical brass quintet consists of two trumpet players, a horn player, a trombone player, and a tuba player. When a brass quintet performs, the two trumpet players usually sit facing the audience, while the horn player faces sideways, with the bell of the horn pointing back toward a wall behind the stage. This seating arrangement causes the horn's bell, where the sound exits, to be angled away from the audience. Therefore, if the horn player has a musical solo and wants to make sure the audience can hear it clearly, the horn player typically ______
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: looks at the sheet music instead of looking at the audience.
shifts position so the bell of the horn points more toward the audience.
instructs the other members of the quintet to play louder.
plays at the same volume as the tuba.

rationale:
Choice B is correct. The horn's bell projects sound; angling it toward the audience during a solo lets the audience hear it more clearly.

Choice A is irrelevant — where the player looks doesn't affect sound projection. Choice C would make the soloist less prominent, not more. Choice D would similarly make the horn less prominent than the soloist needs to be.
